perturbation.original_text: SRP shall provide at its expense, and maintain throughout the term of this Agreement, comprehensive general liability insurance in the amount of Five Million Dollars and No/100's ($5,000,000.00) per occurrence with respect to any liability relating to the activities of SRP in the performance of this Agreement SRP shall supply Logan's with a copy of the Certificate of Insurance naming Logan's as an additional insured. Such policies shall provide for at least fifteen (15) days' written notice to Logan's of the cancellation or substantial modification thereof. Such insurance will be supplemental coverage in excess of the Five Million Dollars ($5,000,000.00) primary coverage provided to SRP and Logan's by the comprehensive liability policy of NMS.
perturbation.changed_text: SRP shall provide general liability insurance with respect to any liability relating to the activities of SRP in the performance of this Agreement. SRP shall supply Logan's with a Certificate of Insurance. Such policies shall provide for notice to Logan's of cancellation thereof.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'comprehensive' and 'in the amount of Five Million Dollars and No/100's ($5,000,000.00) per occurrence', the specific coverage is no longer defined, creating a legal loophole. By removing 'at least fifteen (15) days' written notice' the contract could violate laws related to insurance requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Tennessee Code Annotated § 56-7-116 - Notice of cancellation or nonrenewal
perturbation.law_explanation: Tennessee law requires insurance companies to provide a certain amount of notice before cancelling or not renewing an insurance policy. This modification contradicts that law because it allows for any type of notice or the absence of a notice period, which may be shorter than what is legally required.

perturbation.justification: The omission of 'at least fifteen (15) days' written notice' creates a legal loophole regarding cancellation notice, as per the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation further suggests that Tennessee law requires a certain amount of notice before cancellation, and the modified text could allow for a shorter notice period than legally mandated. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unavailable and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a login page, the combined Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation demonstrate a potential contradiction by omitting a required legal consumer protection. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: Either party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement, subject to the following terms and conditions: (a) Either party ("the Terminating Party") may terminate this Agreement upon prior written notice to the other party (the "Defaulting Party") upon the occurrence of any of the following conditions: (i) In the event that such party materially breaches this Agreement or materially defaults in the performance of any obligation hereunder and fails to cure said breach within 30 days of written notice to the Defaulting Party by the Terminating Party; (ii) Immediately in the event that the Defaulting Party (including, for SRP, the Driver) commits any crime involving moral turpitude or otherwise commits any act or is involved in any situation bringing itself, the Terminating Party or the products of the Terminating Party into public hatred or contempt, or engages in conduct that shocks or insults the community or brings the Terminating Party, or its products into public disrespect, scandal or ridicule; or (iii) In the event the LMSC series at NMS is canceled. (b) In the event Logan's terminates this Agreement pursuant to this Article 10, Logan's shall be entitled to a pro rata refund of amounts prepaid for the current calendar year, with no further obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement. In the event Logan's terminates this Agreement pursuant to this Article 10, Logan's shall determine any refund due.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the defined reasons for termination and the pro rata refund clause grants Logan's significantly more power to terminate the contract without cause or without proper compensation. Tennessee law does not specifically require a pro rata refund in all contract terminations, but this omission removes a previously agreed-upon consumer (SRP) protection, potentially leading to disputes and legal challenges under general contract law principles of fairness and good faith.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Tennessee Consumer Protection Act of 1977, Tenn. Code Ann. § 47-18-101
perturbation.law_explanation: While the TCPA doesn't mandate specific pro rata refunds, it prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. By giving Logan's sole discretion over refunds upon termination, the modified contract creates a situation where Logan's could potentially deny refunds arbitrarily, which could be construed as an unfair or deceptive practice under the TCPA, especially if SRP has fulfilled its obligations up to the point of termination. The act aims to protect consumers from deceptive business practices, and this could be argued as one.

You must accept these Terms & Conditions to use the service. Please also review the Privacy & Cookies policy
perturbation.justification: The original contract included specific conditions for termination and a pro rata refund clause, which provided a degree of consumer protection. The changed text removes these conditions and gives Logan's sole discretion over refunds. Although the provided law snippets are unhelpful, the perturbation explanation, law explanation, and the clear removal of the pro rata refund clause (a previously agreed upon consumer protection) constitute a legal contradiction as an omission. Specifically, this falls under Omission Legal Contradiction, as a legally mandated consumer protection has been removed.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement contains the entire understanding between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior written or oral agreements between them with respect to the subject to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement contains the understanding between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the text 'supersedes all prior written or oral agreements between them with respect to the subject to the subject matter hereof' could allow for previous agreements to still be valid. In Tennessee, the parol evidence rule generally prevents parties from introducing extrinsic evidence of prior agreements to contradict the terms of a fully integrated written contract. By removing the text establishing that this agreement is the 'entire understanding', the enforceability of the contract is weaker.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a codified statute, the parol evidence rule is a well-established principle of contract law in Tennessee (and most jurisdictions). By omitting the 'entire agreement' clause, it creates ambiguity and the potential for prior agreements to be considered, thus undermining the purpose of having a written contract. The case law example from Justia discusses the use of the parol evidence rule in Tennessee. This modified term of the contract allows SRP to make the case that prior agreements are valid.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 47-2-202 - Final written expression Parol or extrinsic evidence. :: 2010 Tennessee Code :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Tennessee Code 2024 Tennessee Code 2023 Tennessee Code 2021 Tennessee Code 2010 Tennessee Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 Tennessee Code Title 47 - Commercial Instruments And Transactions Chapter 2 - A Leases Part 2 - Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract 47-2-202 - Final written expression Parol or extrinsic evidence. 47-2-202. Final written expression Parol or extrinsic evidence. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented: (a) By course of performance, course of dealing or usage of trade, pursuant to § 47-1-303; and (b) By evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. [Acts 1963, ch. 81, § 1 (2-202); Acts 2008, ch. 930, § 2.] Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Tennessee may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The omission of the phrase 'supersedes all prior written or oral agreements' directly contradicts the principle of contract law that aims to prevent the introduction of prior agreements that could contradict the written contract. The scraped law snippet 1, Tennessee Code 47-2-202, addresses the parol evidence rule, stating that terms in a final written agreement 'may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement'. By removing the clause that explicitly states the current agreement supersedes all prior agreements, the changed text allows for the potential introduction of prior agreements, thus weakening the contract's enforceability and contradicting the intent of the parol evidence rule as highlighted in the law explanation.
